text: The video shows a section of the sigmoid colon with a visible puckered area indicative of colon cancer, as the camera focuses on this region.

A five French open-ended catheter is inserted into the right ureter, and five mls of IC green dye is injected to enhance visualization. The ureter is visualized using infrared light, appearing as a green structure, aiding in the lateralization of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon utilizes two robotic arms and two non-robotic laparoscopic ports for enhanced mobility in the surgical field. A bipolar device is used to desiccate a venous branch, while larger vessels are clipped. The procedure involves dissecting deep to reach the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring adequate lymph node sampling for cancer staging.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesentery, revealing the inferior mesenteric artery. Due to its size, the artery is clipped rather than desiccated. The specimen is removed, showing enough redundant colon for a side-to-side anastomosis. The splenic flexure was mobilized initially.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn intracorporeal anastomosis, focusing on the back row of the two-layered anastomosis. The posterior serosal layer is being sutured with interrupted stitches.

The surgeon begins suturing the mucosal layer of the anastomosis using a running stitch technique with 3-0 Vicryl on an SH needle. Canal stitches are applied to the ends and edges of the anastomosis, demonstrating a hand-sewn intracorporeal approach facilitated by robotic assistance.

The surgeon demonstrates the canal suture technique, starting with an outside-in, inside-out pattern at the corners of the anastomosis. The linear portion is completed with a running suture, followed by the serosal layer closure. The mucosal layer is closed first, and the importance of incorporating overlying fat into the serosal layer is discussed.

Ironically, I think a lot of surgeons that do hand sews try to completely remove all that fat. I look at that fat like omentum. And, you know, there's a significant subclinical leak rate with all anastomosis, regardless of whether it's stapled, hand-sewn. I mean, a subclinical patient's never going to declare that they've leaked a little. And I think that the little epiploic fat, like omentum, acts as a barrier when you're doing the anastomosis. So I myself don't mind catching some of the epiploic fat to include that into the anastomosis on the serosal side. layer. Plus, that also helps with coagulation and helps with... Well, if you understand tissue thromboplastins also, if there's any bleeding, I mean, not that it's going to be significant, but yeah, I think it's essentially... a good part of the procedure.

Also fat brings blood supply and if there's little even subclinical leaks I do believe that it will be beneficial. And again your serosal layer here is interrupted.
